coredata.dc:title: Simple synthesis of the Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures electrocatalyst for enhancing the methanol oxidation
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Jianghua
Shao, Tao
Luo, Cong
Li, Jinling
He, Shijie
Meng, Bowen
Zhang, Qiankun
Zhang, Dongxia
Xue, Zhonghua
Zhou, Xibin
coredata.dc:description: Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures with Au-GQDs yolk and AgPt alloy shell have been synthesized through a green wet chemical reduction method. In this process employed Au-GQDs as a yolk to obtain Au-GQDs@Ag nanoparticles (NPs), and then galvanic replacement reaction occurred between PtCl6 2- and Ag to fabricate Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures. GQDs served as a structure-directing and dispersing agent to prepare homogeneous shell. The electrocatalytic performance of the as-synthesized Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures for methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) is studied by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and chronoamperometry (CA). The catalyst has displayed excellent specific activity (33.20 mA cm−2), which is approximately 7 times higher than those of commercial Pt/C. And its CO tolerance and stability for MOR are also greatly improved. The results attribute to their particular structure (Yolk-shell nanostructures), electron effect with Ag and Pt, electron transport between GQDs and AgPt shell. The present strategy can provide an environment-friendly and valuable way to fabricate multicomponent nanostructures with Yolk-shell nanostructures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Yolk-shell nanostructures
Methanol oxidation
Electrocatalytic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical performance of Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures electrocatalyst

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of GQDs; inset: size distribution of GQDs. (b) TEM image of Au-GQDs; inset: size distribution of Au-GQDs. (c) HRTEM image of single Au-GQDs. (d) SAED patterns of Au-GQDs.

(a) TEM image of Au-GQDs@Ag NPs (Au-GQDs yolk and Ag shells are pointed by yellow and red arrows); inset: size distribution of Au-GQDs@Ag NPs. (b) HRTEM image of Au-GQDs@Ag NPs. (c) SAED patterns of Au-GQDs@Ag NPs. (d) Elemental line scanning over the entire particle region indicated by the red line panel; inset: HAADF-STEM image of Au-GQDs@Ag NPs. (e) HAADF-STEM elemental mapping images of an Au-GQDs@Ag NPs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) TEM images of Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostuctures. (b) HRTEM images of Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostuctures. (c) SAED patterns of Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostuctures. (d) Elemental line scanning over the entire particle region indicated by the red line panel; inset: HAADF-STEM image of Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostuctures. (e) EDX results (c) of an Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostuctures (note that the Cu peaks come from the copper grid). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
XRD patterns of Au-GQDs@Ag NPs and Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostuctures.
XPS patterns of (a) C1s, (b) O1s, (c) Au 4f, (d) Ag 3d and (e) Pt 4f for Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostuctures.
UV–vis absorption spectra of (a) Au-GQDs NPs. (b) Au-GQDs@Ag NPs. (c) Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostuctures.

(a) CV curves of Au-GQDs@AgPt, Au-GQDs@Pt, Au@Pt and Pt/C catalysts modified electrodes in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with a scan rate of 50 mVs−1; (b) CV and (c) histogram curves of Au-GQDs@AgPt, Au-GQDs@Pt, Au@Pt and Pt/C catalysts modified electrodes in 1.0 M NaOH solution containing 0.5 M methanol; (d) CA curves of Au-GQDs@AgPt, Au-GQDs@Pt, Au@Pt and Pt/C catalysts modified electrodes in 1.0 M NaOH solution containing 0.5 M methanol.
Schematic illustration demonstrates the structural and compositional features of the porosity Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostuctures as an efficient MOR catalyst.
The synthesis process of Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures.
Binding energies of Pt 4f and atomic compositions of different Pt species.

head.ce:title: Simple synthesis of the Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures electrocatalyst for enhancing the methanol oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Geography and Environment Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province
College of Geography and Environment Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Geography and Environment Science, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, 730070, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, 730070, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures with Au-GQDs yolk and AgPt alloy shell have been synthesized through a green wet chemical reduction method. In this process employed Au-GQDs as a yolk to obtain Au-GQDs@Ag nanoparticles (NPs), and then galvanic replacement reaction occurred between PtCl6 2- and Ag to fabricate Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures. GQDs served as a structure-directing and dispersing agent to prepare homogeneous shell. The electrocatalytic performance of the as-synthesized Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures for methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) is studied by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and chronoamperometry (CA). The catalyst has displayed excellent specific activity (33.20 mA cm−2), which is approximately 7 times higher than those of commercial Pt/C. And its CO tolerance and stability for MOR are also greatly improved. The results attribute to their particular structure (Yolk-shell nanostructures), electron effect with Ag and Pt, electron transport between GQDs and AgPt shell. The present strategy can provide an environment-friendly and valuable way to fabricate multicomponent nanostructures with Yolk-shell nanostructures.

Schematic illustration demonstrates the structural and compositional features of the porosity Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures as an efficient MOR catalyst.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: For the past few years, direct methanol oxidation fuel cell (DMFCs) as clean energy sources have attracted a number of interests because of its low working temperature, high energy-transport efficiency, suitable power density, and low emission of pollutants [1–4]. Noble metal catalysts, basically platinum are diffusely used as anode catalyst for MOR due to their most efficient catalysis activity and practical performances [5]. However, the self-poisoning resulting in a poor CO-tolerance, the large consumption of Pt materials and costly, severely hinder the commercialization of DMFCs [6]. Hence, tremendous efforts have been dedicated to the development of higher utilization efficiency with Pt-based catalysts in the field of electrocatalysis.

Up to date, in many reported the Pt-based catalysts, the Pt-based multi-metallic core-shell catalysts have attracted more attention due to their higher catalytic activity than pure Pt catalyst and their low cost [7,8]. Sliver (Ag) has been identified as a candidate metal to alloy with Pt because of: i) Ag and Pt have alike lattice constants, which is favorable for the growth of the AgPt alloy formation [9]; ii) Ag is cheaper than Pt, more abundant in nature. The synergistic effect of the interaction with Ag and Pt changes the structure of the Pt electron band by altering the surface adsorption force, which helps to significantly improve the electrocatalytic activity and stability [10]; iii) the doped Ag can stabilize Pt, release the active sites of Pt previously occupied by the poisoning CO-like intermediates, and eventually enhance the catalytic ability [11]. For example, Liu’s group facilely synthesized porous PtAg/reduced graphene oxide alloy nanoflowers for highly efficient ORR and hydrogen evolution [11]. Han et al. fabricated Ag@PdPt core-shell NPs with dendritic Pd–Pt shells, and the Ag@PdPt NPs also showed excellent electrocatalytic activity for MOR [12]. Recently, Wang et al. [13] have reported the use of 3D freestanding porous PtAg hollow chain-like networks for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in acid media with increased catalytic activity. Additionally, in recent years, Pt-based bimetallic catalysts acquired by mixing with metal Au have also been developed to improve catalytic activity. First, Au is a particular metal because it is inert in the bulk state but has good electrical conductivity and excellent catalytic activity [14]. And Au can improve the concentration active oxygen species, which can increase interactions between active-OH and CO-like species and effectively enhance the resistance to poisoning and durability of the catalyst [15]. Second, Au can make d-band center shift and lattice shrinking of Pt, which can weaken the adsorption of various intermediates [16–18]. Tao and his colleague successfully synthesized the Au@AgPd core-shell NPs [19], which exhibited high electrocatalytic activity for methanol oxidation.

Moreover, the inherent nanopores and hollow structures of porous or spongy-like nanostructures can broaden the high surface area and allow the target molecules to readily penetrate or support the transport of fuel to the active reaction sites within the catalyst, thereby improving the activity properties. For instance, Yolk-shell nanostructures with interior yolk, void space, and permeable exterior shell are core@shell nanostructures [20]. In the recent years, Yolk-shell nanostructures have adsorbed more and more attentions for catalysis, energy, and environment varying applications including catalysis and energy storage due to their high chemical activity, special surface area and high degree of roughness [21].

In addition, to prevent aggregation, minimize the employment of the noble metals and improve the catalytic activity, a wide range of support materials including graphene [22,23], porous carbon [24,25], carbon nanosheets [26], carbon quantum dots [23,27], carbon nanotubes [28] as well as other substrates [29] have been extensively employed to enhance the stability of the NPs. Thus far, GQDs, being made up of a single atomic layer of graphite, have high conductivity, special surface area and better surface self-assembly by π-π conjugation and surface groups [30–32]. Intriguingly, these GQDs supports materials have been shown to interact electronically with metal NPs to adjust the MOR activity of metal NPs [33,34]. Due to their large surface-volume ratio, such GQDs are anticipated to display rich structural defects, which may be exploited to facilitate electron transfer from Pt to methanol as well as to control the combining of reaction CO-like species on the Pt surface, as illustrated in a recent theoretical study [35].

Herein, in this paper, we used the literature to fabricate GQDs [36] and then used them as supporting substrates for Au NPs. Following, we reported a simple galvanic replacement reaction approach to prepare Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures with an AgPt alloy shell and Au-GQDs yolk. The Au-GQDs acted as yolk to synthesize Au-GQDs@Ag NPs. Then the Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures were obtained by galvanic replacement reaction at 25 °C. At the same time, GQDs acted as directing agent and dispersing agent to obtain the homogeneous shell. The synthesized strategy is depicted in Scheme 1 . Moreover, the obtained Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures show better electrocatalytic activity and stability than Au-GQDs@Pt, Au@Pt and Pt/C.

Sodium citrate dehydrate (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O) and Silver nitrate (AgNO3) were provided via Tianjin Kaixin Chemical Reagent Company. Graphite was obtained from Shanpu Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), Chloroplatinic acid hexahydrate (H2PtCl6·6H2O) and Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was supplied by Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%) 、Sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) and Nitric acid (HNO3) were acquired from Beijing Chemical Factory. Commercial Pt/C (20% wt) was purchased from McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). And all chemicals and reagents were analytical grade in this work. The water used in this paper was Twice-di-water.

The morphology and structure of the samples were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI TECNAI20, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was detected on a Panalytical X-pert diffractometer with Cu Kα1 radiation (λ = 0.154056 nm). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was probed with ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was acquired with an Emispec Vision Software (FEI Company). Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis) was calculated by a Cary 500 Scan UV–vis–near-infrared spectrometer.

On the base of the previous report [36], 300 mg graphite powder were dispersed in mixed acid (containing concentrated HNO3 20 mL and concentrated H2SO4 60 mL) in a round-bottom flask. Then, the above solution was reacted for 12 h at 120 °C. After cooling down 25 °C, dilute the solution by pouring it into 300 ml di-water, followed by neutralizing the acid with Na2CO3. Then Na2SO4 and NaNO3 salt were removed from the solution at ice water bath by 220 nm filter membrane. Lastly, the obtained solution was dialyzed using 3500Da dialysis bag.

Au-GQDs were prepared as described in previous report [37]. The above Au-GQDs NPs solution (10 mL) was added into a round-bottom flask (50 mL). Then 160 μL AA (0.1 M) was put into the Au-GQDs NPs solution. Following 120 μL AgNO3 (0.01 M) were added into the above solution. Lastly, the Au-GQDs@Ag NPs were synthesized in above solutions at room temperature for 2 h. The suspension was washed by Twice-di-water.

The Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures catalyst was synthesized by a simple replacement reaction as follows: Briefly, H2PtCl6·6H2O (200 μL, 25.4 mM) was added into the as-prepared Au-GQDs@Ag NPs solution (10 mL) with a gentle shaking and reaction was kept for 2 h at room temperature. The Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures were performed by the galvanic replacement reaction occurred between PtCl6 2- and Ag shell. The by-products were cleaned by centrifuging at a speed of 12000 rpm for 10 min, and then cleaned three times with alcohol and water (1:1). The Au-GQDs@Pt, Au@Pt and Pt/C catalysts were prepared with the same procedure.

The electrochemical experiments were detected using a CHI660B electrochemical workstation and a conventional three-electrode cell with Pt wire as the counter electrode, the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as reference electrode, and the modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, ∼3 mm diameter) as working electrode. In this work, in order to modify working electrode, 2 mg prepared Au-GQDs@AgPt, Au-GQDs@Pt, Au@Pt and Pt/C dispersed in 2 mL water to generate a homogeneous ink, respectively. Then 5 μL of the ink (1 mg/mL) was dropped onto a cleaned GCE and dried at room temperature. The methanol oxidation was performed in 0.5 M H2SO4 and 1.0 M NaOH+0.5 M methanol solution. The electrolyte solution was purged with N2 for 10min.

The morphologies of the GQDs and Au-GQDs samples are observed with TEM. From Fig. 1 (a), it can be clearly seen the prepared GQDs with an average diameter 6.0 ± 0.03 nm dispersed uniformly. The average diameter of Au-GQDs measured by TEM image was 11.6 ± 0.4 nm and the shape was nearly similar to the spherical, as shown in Fig. 1(b). As shown in Fig. 1(c), the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of Au-GQDs exhibits clear lattice fringes separated by 0.235 nm and 0.238 nm, corresponding to the face-centered cubic (fcc) Au {111} and graphite {100}, respectively. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) of Au-GQDs displays several bright consecutive concentric rings (Fig. 1(d)) ascribe to the diffusions from the {111},{200},{220} and {311} planes of fcc Au and the {100}, {101} planes of fcc graphite.

As seen in Fig. 2 (a), the Au-GQDs@Ag NPs are spherical shape without agglomeration. The average size of these NPs is ca.20.0 nm. And the core-shell structure of Au-GQDs@Ag NPs was displayed by HRTEM imaging (Fig. 2(b)). The obvious bright/dark contrast clearly confirms the existence of the core-shell structure. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the lattice spacings are about 0.236 nm were found in the edge region of the core-shell Au-GQDs@Ag NPs, which are indexed to the (111) plane of fcc Ag (0.2359 nm) [35]. The corresponding SAED pattern is shown in Fig. 2(c). The present of distinct points, corresponding to the (111) planes of fcc Ag, manifests that the Ag NPs is single-crystalline. The cross-sectional compositional line profiles reveal the presence of an Au-GQDs core and an Ag shell in the Au-GQDs@Ag NPs (see inset Fig. 2(d)). The elemental line-scanning profiles revealed that the element Au forms the core and distributes the center; The element Ag forms the shell and it distributes the external surface, which confirms the core-shell structure of Au-GQDs@Ag NPs (see Fig. 2(d)). Moreover, as shown in Fig. 2(e), the high-angle annular dark-field scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM) elemental mapping images also further demonstrate the core-shell structure of Au-GQDs@Ag NPs.

Moreover, the structure features of the as-synthesized Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures were first observed by TEM. As seen in Fig. 3 (a), the as-prepared products are relatively uniform structures with an average particle diameter of 23.20 nm, and almost no other byproducts are found. The fringes with lattice spacing of 0.230 nm, 0.229 nm and 0.231 nm are located to the fcc Pt {111} (0.2265 nm) and Ag {111} (0.2359 nm) planes, indicating the synthesis of AgPt shell, as shown in Fig. 3(b). In addition, the SEAD pattern shows that the Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures are polycrystalline (Fig. 3(c)). Meanwhile, the Au elements diffused to inner core and AgPt components oppositely diffused to outer surface, analyzed by EDX line scanning profiles (Fig. 3(d)), which further confirmed the successful synthesis of Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures.

The composition of the Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures was further studyed by XRD [38]. As seen in Fig. 4 , the Au-GQD@Ag, Au-GQDs@AgPt displayed a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ in range of 20°–30°,which may be assigned to the graphite (002) planes, manifesting the GQDs is existed in catalysts [37]. And from Fig. 4, it is obvious that the Au-GQDs@Ag NPs have clear diffraction peaks at 38.3°, 44.6°, 64.8° and 77.7°, and Au-GQDs@AgPt have apparent diffraction peaks at 39.85°, 45.8°, 66.3° and 80.7°, which well assigned to the (111), (200), (220) and (311) planes of typical face-centered-cubic (fcc) Au structure, respectively. The results obviously indicate that the diffraction peaks of Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures occurred shifts, suggesting the electronic effect came from the electrons transfer from Pt to Au-GQDs. Besides, further observation of (111) peak, it is clearly that Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures suggest a broad peak at 2θ = 39-40° which is located between the Ag and Pt component, and the diffraction peaks of Au-GQDs@AgPt shift to high angles. The above results indicate the synthesization of the AgPt alloy. At the same time, the diffraction peak appears between standard peaks of Pt (JCPDS no. 04-0802) and Ag (JCPDS no. 04-0783) [39,40], further proving the synthesization of AgPt alloy [41,42].

The elemental composition of the Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures was investigated via XPS measurements [43]. As observed in Fig. 5 (a), C1s is divided into three peaks, assigning to the CC (284eV), C–O (286eV) and O–CO (289eV) [37]. Fig. 5(b) displays O1s is divided into two distinct peaks, which is corresponded to the CO (532.3eV) and C–O/C–O–C (533eV) [44]. And as shown in Fig. 5(c), Au 4f5/2 and Au 4f7/2 peaks at 87.48 and 83.80 eV are corresponded to Au0. This indicates the whole reduction of AuCl4 − to Au0 atoms [45]. It can see from Fig. 5(d) that the spectrum of Ag 3d can be split into one pair of peaks, which are ascribed to Ag0. It indicates the primary species of Ag0 in the sample [46]. In the meantime, as can be seen from Fig. 5(e), the Pt 4f is divided into two pair of diffraction peaks. And the weaker pair at 75.49 and 71.81 eV are corresponded to Pt2+, whereas the stronger couples at 74.28 and 70.97 eV are ascribed to Pt0 [47], as summarized in Table 1 . Moreover, the above results relative to the pure Pt (70.90 and 74.25 eV) and pure Ag (367.90 and 373.80 eV), the binding energy of Pt has positive shifts, while the binding energy of Ag has negative shifts. At the same time, the binding energies of Pt0 have positive shifts in comparison with that of the Au-GQDs@Pt [37]. The shifts of the binding energy suggest that the electron transport from Pt to Ag related to electronic interaction between Pt and Ag and further demonstrates the formation of AgPt alloy [48,49].

Additionally, as shown in Fig. 6 , the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Au-GQDs、Au-GQDs@Ag and Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures. The Au-GQDs clearly showed a characteristic absorption peak located at 528 nm, which ascribed to the surface plasmon resonance of Au NPs (see Fig. 6(a)). As seen in Fig. S1, the typical absorption peak at 230 nm could be assigned to the π-π∗ transition of graphitic sp2 domains. It could be seen the absorption of Au-GQDs has a blue shift [50]. As seen in Fig. 6(b), the absorption spectra shifted from 520 nm to 400 nm indicates that the Ag was successfully encapsulated on the Au-GQDs NPs surface. With the galvanic replacement reaction happened after the addition of PtCl6 2-, the absorption peak occurred red shift and was gradually suppressed (see Fig. 6(c)), because of the increasing AgPt alloy shell [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures electrocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic property of Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures for MOR was tested using CV at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 and at 25 °C. As seen in Fig. 7 , the different catalysts are compared for methanol oxidation.

The Electrochemical Active Surface Area (ECSA) could be estimated from the following Eq. (1) [52].
E C S A =
Q H
0.21 ×
M
P t
Where QH (in mC cm−2) is the charge exchanged during the electro-desorption of hydrogen on Pt. 0.21 mC−2 is the electrical charge assumed for hydrogen adsorption on the surface of Pt. The MPt denotes the amount of Pt loaded on the GC electrode. It can be seen from the Fig. 7(a), the CV of Au@Pt, Au-GQDs@Pt, Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures and Pt/C electrodes in 0.5 M H2SO4. The ECSA values of the Au@Pt, Au-GQDs@Pt, Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures and Pt/C catalysts were calculated to be 98.86 m2g-1, 116.32m2g-1, 261.29m2g-1 and 19.86m2g-1, respectively. Owing to large hollows on the AgPt shells, both the exterior and interior surfaces of AgPt shells can be capitalized on catalysis, thus resulting in very large ECSA. This indicates that the Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures have more excellent active surface area and catalytic activity than the other nanocatalysts. Meanwhile, we have tested the ECSA curves of Au-GQDs@AgPt, Au-GQDs@Pt, Au@Pt and Pt/C in nitrogen-saturated 1.0 M NaOH from −1.0 V to 0.4 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 (Fig. S2). The current peaks between −1.0 V and −0.6 V were ascribed to hydrogen adsorption/desorption at the Pt catalysts [53]. The ECSA was estimated by.Eq. (1) The calculated ECSA of the prepared Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell、Au-GQDs@Pt、Au@Pt and Pt/C were 241.32 m2g-1、103.22m2g-1、76.58m2g-1 and 12.82m2g-1, respectively. The above results exhibited that Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell had a larger double-layer capacitance than that of the Au-GQDs@Pt, Au@Pt and Pt/C electrodes, further indicating that the ECSA of the Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell was larger than that of the Au-GQDs@Pt, Au@Pt and Pt/C electrodes [54]. Although the mechanism of MOR is different from acidic solution and alkaline solution, the above results show that Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell has a large ECSA. This larger ECSA was likely due to the high surface area and porous structure of the Yolk-shell. The electro-catalytic activities of Au-GQDs@Pt, Au@AgPt, and Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures for methanol oxidation were tested using CV as seen in Fig. 7(b). The experiments were performed in 1.0 M NaOH solution containing 0.5 M CH3OH. The CV scans were carried within a potential range of −0.8 V–0.4 V at 50 mV s−1. It can be seen from Fig. 7(b) that the Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures have a lower onset potential of methanol oxidation. It suggested that on the Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures surface, methanol is more easily oxidized. In addition, the peak current density of Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures (33.20 mA cm−2) is very larger than those of Au-GQDs@Pt (15.68 mA cm−2), Au@Pt (8.91 mA cm−2) and Pt/C (3.65 mA cm−2), displaying indeed an outstanding electro-catalytic activity of the Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures over the three nanocatalysts. This is consistent with the CV histogram results of Fig. 7(c). Moreover, Fig. S3(a) shows that the catalyst of Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell has a highest catalytic activity and the current density is up to 2265.11 mA mgPt −1. Meanwhile, the peak current density of the four catalysts toward MOR is displayed by the histograms of mass activity in Fig. S3(b). And the electrocatalytic activity of four catalysts displays a similar trend between the specific activity and mass activity. Specially, the Au-GQD@Ag has no electro-catalytic activity for methanol oxidation. From the data in Table S1, our values are comparable with other results obtained for MOR in alkaline media. It was found that the Au-GQDs@AgPt catalyst has a higher current density peak compared with the reported catalysts. Those above results demonstrated that the unique structure of Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures exposes large active surface areas, providing abundant active sites for the MOR.

For the purpose of further studying the Au-GQDs@Pt, Au@Pt, Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures and Pt/C catalysts of stability to methanol oxidation, CA tests were investigated in 1.0 M NaOH containing 0.5 M CH3OH solution for 3000 s at a speed rate of 50 mV s−1. Fig. 7(d) shows that the current decreased rapidly at beginning stage, which because of catalysts poisoning by the accumulation of intermediate species such as COads on the Pt active sites [55,56]. And after the long-time reaction, the currents of all catalysts trended to stabilize. After 3000s, the Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures showed the most excellent current density with the low decay rate than the other catalysts, demonstrating that the Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures can greatly enhance the electrocatalytic activity, stability and CO-poisoning tolerance of catalysts. Finally, in order to observe whether the structure and morphology of Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell has changed after a long-term CA test. We have characterized Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructure after 3000s CA test. From the TEM image (Fig. S4), we found that although the Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell sample is a little agglomerated, the Yolk-shell structure can still be clearly seen after 3000s CA test.

The kinetics and interfacial process of electrode reactions have been investigated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to evaluate the electrochemical properties of the as-prepared catalysts. EIS were carried out for the Au-GQDs@AgPt, Au-GQDs@Pt, Au@Pt and Pt/C electrodes at onset potential of methanol oxidation in 0.5 M CH3OH and 1.0 M NaOH. The Nyquist plots are shown in Fig. S5. As seen from the figure, the diameter of the impedance arc (DIA) of the as-prepared catalysts decreases in the order: Pt/C, Au@Pt, Au-GQDs@Pt and Au-GQDs@AgPt. Smaller diameter of the arc indicates lower charge transfer resistance (Rct) for MOR. The Rct values are ∼47.60Ω, ∼59.07Ω, ∼90.37Ω and ∼206Ω for Au-GQDs@AgPt, Au-GQDs@Pt, Au@Pt and Pt/C, respectively. Compared to the above four catalysts, Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell exhibited the smaller DIA, which further demonstrates the higher catalytic activity of Au-GQDs@AgPt for MOR [57].

Based on the above experimental results, Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures shows better electrochemical activity, CO tolerances ability, and durability that the Au-GQDs@Pt,Au@Pt and Pt/C electro-catalysts. This may be because of the following reasons: (1) GQDs might function as both dispersing agents and supports for the nucleation and growth of AgPt shell due to the many active sites from various oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface; (2) GQDs with a good electronic conductivity can promote the transfer of electrons between AgPt catalysts and methanol, and reduce the electrode resistance. Additionally, the existence of Au-GQDs yolk inside a porous shell activates the Pt catalytic sites by the electronic effect derived from the electrons transfer from Pt to Au-GQDs [58,59]. And because the Fermi-energy of the two metals can be different, therefore accelerating the cleaving dynamics of the C–H bond; (3) According to the bifunctional mechanism [60–62]: the addition Ag activates water activation, which generates more-OH or other oxygen-containing species to oxidize CO like intermediates on Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures catalyst surface; (4)This unique Yolk-shell nanostructures provides an indeed opportunity to integrate the functions of both shells and cores into unprecedented synergetic collective properties, which afford more active reaction sites in methanol oxidation, as proved Fig. 8 .

In short, Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures catalyst was prepared by an effective and green wet chemical reduction method, which has a superior electrochemical stability and a significantly enhances catalytic for MOR. In addition, the catalytic activity of Au-GQDs@AgPt Yolk-shell nanostructures was 33.20 mA m−2, which is about 7 times as high as that of commercial Pt/C. It should be mainly attributed to its unique Yolk-shell nanostructure (large cavity and porous AgPt alloy shell), which not only provided many active sites, but also significantly improved the electrocatalysis activity, by electron effect with Ag and Pt, GQDs and AgPt shell. These Yolk-shell nanostructures are suitable for the applications in DMFC.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Jianghua Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Tao Shao: Software. Cong Luo: Writing - review & editing. Jinling Li: Writing - review & editing. Shijie He: Writing - review & editing. Bowen Meng: Writing - review & editing. Qiankun Zhang: Writing - review & editing. Dongxia Zhang: Writing - review & editing. Xibin Zhou: Writing - review & editing, Validation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21165016, 21175108, and 21265018) and the Science and Technology Support Projects of Gansu Province (No.s 1011GKCA025, 090GKCA036, 1208RJZM289) and the Innovation Capacity Improvement Plan of Northwest Normal University (CX2018Y007).
